data.title: Ensign Energy Services GAAP EPS of -C$0.21
data.description:
• Ensign Energy Services press release (ESVIF): FY GAAP EPS of -C$0.21.
  • Revenue for 2025 was C$1,638.9 million, a three percent decrease from 2024 revenue of C$1,684.2 million.

data.title: DraftKings and Disney strike a key integration deal ahead of March Madness
data.description:
DraftKings (DKNG) and Disney's (DIS) ESPN announced on Friday that they will enable customers to link their DraftKings Sportsbook and ESPN accounts to deliver a more personalized, streamlined betting and viewing experience. The feature was added just ahead of the men’s and women’s March Madness tournaments, which recently reached their highest viewership in over 30 years. 

Once live, users can link accounts via a new "Bet Your Bracket" tab in the DraftKings (DKNG) app. Fans who create an ESPN Tournament Challenge bracket will then see betting options tailored to their bracket picks, along with customized suggestions and engagement opportunities throughout the tournament, plus one free month of ESPN Unlimited for those who link. 

The move is seen by both companies as a strategic step toward deeper integration, with DraftKings (DKNG) positioning it as part of its upcoming Super App strategy and ESPN highlighting future features such as in-app and web tracking of upcoming, live, and settled bets and personalized promotions based on users’ favorite teams, players, and fantasy rosters. 

Shares of DraftKings (DKNG) fell 1.6% on the day, while ESPN parent Disney (DIS) was down 1.7%.

data.title: Medpace, IQVIA upgraded at Barclays; says AI concerns are overdone
data.description:
As part of a broader review of life science and diagnostic tools stocks, Barclays upgraded contract research organizations IQVIA Holdings (IQV) and Medpace Holdings (MEDP) on Friday, noting that concerns over the AI-related disruption in the sector may be overdone.

Medpace (MEDP) has lost more than 46% over the past 12 months, while IQVIA (IQV) has shed over 5% during the same period. “We think the sell-off has reset multiples to attractive levels and presents good entry points for both short-term and longer-term investors," Bloomberg reported, quoting Barclays analyst Luke Sergott.

However, Sergott downgraded life sciences companies Avantor (AVTR) and Pacific Biosciences of California (PACB) to Underweight from Equal-weight. “AI likely has more impact on CROs than core tools, but we still think fears over AI’s disruption to CROs are overblown,” the analyst added as he upgraded IQVIA (IQV) and Medpace (MEDP) to Overweight from Equal-weight.

“We think investor fears and the drop in IQV shares are overdone,” Sergott wrote on IQVIA (IQV), and he opined that Medpace (MEDP) stock warrants a premium and “it has meaningfully closed the gap vs. peers and is back to normal levels.”

Regarding Avantor (AVTR), the analyst sees “less upside to shares vs. the group and thinks it may be some time before AVTR catches up as the turnaround story slowly takes shape over the course of many incremental announcements in the coming year((s)).”

Sergott also downgraded AI-driven drug discovery firm Certara (CERT) to Equal Weight from Overweight, noting, “It will be some time before we see durable valuation unlock in shares.”

data.title: Granite Ridge Resources targets 9% production growth in 2026 while reducing capital spending
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Ridge Resources (GRNT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Tyler Farquharson, CEO & President, highlighted a strategic shift from a traditional nonoperated model to a capital allocator focused on the Permian Basin, stating this "is driving force behind our results." He announced average daily production increased 27% year-over-year to 35,100 barrels of oil equivalent per day for Q4, with total production for the year up similarly to 32,000 barrels per day. Adjusted EBITDAX for the quarter was approximately $70 million, and capital expenditures were $127.5 million for Q4. The company maintained its quarterly dividend of $0.11 per share.
  • Farquharson noted partnerships with four operator teams in the Permian, underscoring a scalable operator partnership strategy and proprietary deal flow, reviewing nearly 700 opportunities and investing $122 million across 107 transactions in 2025.
  • He introduced Kyle Kettler as the new CFO, following a six-month search: "We couldn't be happier that Kyle decided to join us. He brings significant capital markets expertise and extensive network and a keen strategic perspective, that will be critical as we transition towards sustainable free cash flow in the next phase of Granite Ridge's development."
  • R. Kettler, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Granite Ridge is building something truly different, allocating capital and creating value from a platform that's unique in public and private E&P. I'm excited to be here."
  • Kettler added, "For the fourth quarter, oil and natural gas sales totaled $105.5 million. Revenue was essentially flat compared to the prior year quarter because of commodity pricing. However, production grew an impressive 27% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • The company expects annual production in 2026 to average 35,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, representing a 9% increase over 2025. Exit production in 2026 is projected to be essentially flat or modestly up from 2025. Oil volumes are forecast at approximately 51% of total production.
  • Development capital expenditures are projected at $315 million, with an additional $20 million to $30 million earmarked for acquisitions in the pipeline. Approximately 90% of 2026 capital will be focused on operated projects.
  • Management anticipates spending roughly 15% less than last year to achieve the targeted 9% production growth. At current strip pricing, a modest outspend is expected in 2026, with a path to free cash flow from operations in 2027.
  • The company estimates maintenance capital at approximately $250 million for 2026.
  • In light of recent geopolitical shocks, the company has added oil hedges and will continue to monitor the market.

Financial Results

  • Oil and natural gas sales for Q4 totaled $105.5 million, with an average realized oil price of $55.49 per barrel. Natural gas averaged $1.81 per Mcf in the quarter.
  • Adjusted EBITDAX for Q4 was $69.5 million, and operating cash flow totaled $64.5 million. For the full year, oil and natural gas sales were $450.3 million, with production increasing 28% year-over-year to 31,984 barrels equivalent per day. Full year adjusted EBITDAX was $315 million, and operating cash flow was $296.4 million.
  • Lease operating expense in Q4 was $7.72 per barrel equivalent, higher than the previous year due to increased focus on the Permian Basin. Full year LOE averaged $7.27 per barrel equivalent.
  • Liquidity at year-end was $339.5 million, with net debt to adjusted EBITDAX at 1.2x.

Q&A

  • Phillips Johnston, Capital One, asked about Q4 realized oil and gas prices and differentials for 2026. R. Kettler explained weak natural gas realization was driven by Waha pricing and that future modeling uses Waha strip, with anticipated tightening later in the year, while oil differentials were modeled into forecasts.
  • Johnston inquired about the number and mix of net wells for 2026. Kettler responded, "we see 2026 being about 29 net wells coming online and the relative mix of gas and oil should tilt back towards oil as the year goes on."
  • Derrick Whitfield, Texas Capital, sought clarity on the company's 2027 free cash flow objective. Farquharson stated, "it's not an opportunity set driver. It is a leverage driver. We've been very consistent about we want to run the business to 1x to 1.25x or so leverage just to execute the base business plan."
  • Jarrod Giroue, Stephens, asked about the use of free cash flow in 2027. Farquharson noted that options are open, and the focus is on transitioning to a more durable, long-term business.
  • Noah Hungness, BofA Securities, questioned inventory competitiveness and oil production cadence. Farquharson replied, "that opportunity still exists for us. Our operator teams are still executing on transactions that look exactly like that," and stated, "exit-to-exit oil production growth is 12%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on pricing, capital allocation, inventory growth, and strategic transitions, with questions reflecting interest in operational execution and capital discipline.
  • Management presented a confident outlook during prepared remarks, emphasizing growth, capital efficiency, and strategic positioning. In the Q&A, tone remained measured, with occasional emphasis on discipline and flexibility. Farquharson and Kettler consistently referenced leverage targets and adaptability to market conditions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence was sustained, with greater focus on transition to cash flow generation and capital discipline, while analysts maintained steady interest in growth and returns without notable shifts in skepticism or concern.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The transition from growth to capital efficiency and free cash flow generation is more pronounced in Q4, with explicit targets for 2026 and a stated intent to reach free cash flow from operations in 2027.
  • Guidance for 2026 production and capital expenditures is firmer and more detailed, with a focus on cost alignment and reduced capital intensity versus Q3’s more general outline.
  • Management language around leverage and capital allocation is more prominent, reflecting a maturing business model and planning horizon.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general platform growth and partnership progress in Q3 to specifics on capital deployment, inventory scalability, and timing of cash flow inflection in Q4.
  • Key metrics, such as production growth and development capital, are now articulated with greater clarity, as is the operational impact of strategic partnerships.
  • Management sentiment evolved from optimistic about scaling partnerships in Q3 to highlighting disciplined growth and cash flow transition in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Commodity price volatility, particularly in the Permian Basin, was cited as impacting realized prices and revenue, with management adding oil hedges as a mitigation measure.
  • Increased lease operating expenses, driven by saltwater disposal costs in the Permian, remain a cost focus.
  • Flexibility in capital deployment and development schedules is retained, with management prepared to adjust if oil prices fall below $60 per barrel for a sustained period.
  • Analyst concerns centered on pricing differentials, inventory competitiveness, and timing of free cash flow generation, with management emphasizing risk-adjusted returns and leverage discipline.

Final Takeaway

Granite Ridge Resources describes 2025 as a transformational year, emphasizing a shift from scaling operator partnerships and rapid production growth to a disciplined, capital-efficient approach targeting sustainable free cash flow by 2027. The company plans for 9% production growth in 2026 while reducing development spending, maintaining operational flexibility, and preserving a conservative leverage profile. Strategic partnerships, proprietary deal flow, and a focus on capital allocation in the Permian Basin underpin Granite Ridge’s outlook as it navigates evolving market conditions and positions itself for durable shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quanex outlines $1.84B–$1.87B sales target and margin expansion plans amid operational stabilization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanex Building Products Corporation (NX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • George Wilson, President, CEO & Chairman, opened by noting the retirement of Susan Davis from the Board, acknowledging her impact during the company’s transformation. Wilson stated that "market conditions remained soft and company performance was in line with our expectations," attributing challenges to seasonality, global macroeconomic pressures, and especially end consumer confidence. He highlighted that "inflationary pressures, labor costs and certain raw material costs have started to moderate, energy prices have risen" and referenced "heightened geopolitical tensions" as contributing to a cautious consumer environment.

  • Wilson described progress in the Hardware Solutions segment, reporting that the Monterrey, Mexico plant is now stable and that "we don't expect to provide updates on this matter going forward." In Extruded Solutions, focus has been on product development and repositioning Schlegel Seals, while the Custom Solutions segment prioritizes operational efficiencies and market share integration. Wilson stressed, "these efforts position the Custom Solutions segment to deliver improved performance while building a stronger foundation for sustainable growth."

  • On corporate strategy, Wilson outlined the formation of new commercial and operational excellence teams targeting "new market development, the creation of global pricing strategies, logistics and sourcing projects to drive savings ongoing ERP rationalization and AI-led process improvements." He emphasized continued disciplined debt reduction and the pursuit of "targeted small bolt-on acquisitions if available."

  • Scott Zuehlke, Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "on a consolidated basis, we reported net sales of $409.1 million during the first quarter of 2026," and explained the increase was mainly due to foreign exchange translation and tariff pass-throughs. Zuehlke reported a net loss of $4.1 million or ($0.09) per diluted share, and, on an adjusted basis, a net loss of $0.3 million or ($0.01) per diluted share. He attributed decreased earnings to "reduced operating leverage from lower volumes related to ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty coupled with low consumer confidence and higher, but temporary operational costs related to our hardware plant in Monterrey, Mexico."

Outlook

  • The company provided fiscal 2026 guidance: "on a consolidated basis for fiscal 2026, we estimate that we will generate net sales of $1.84 billion to $1.87 billion, which we expect will yield approximately $240 million to $245 million in adjusted EBITDA." Management expects "gross margin of 28% to 28.5%, SG&A of $295 million to $300 million... D&A of $105 million to $110 million, interest expense of $50 million, a tax rate of about 24%, CapEx of $70 million to $75 million and free cash flow of approximately $100 million."

  • For Q2 2026, Zuehlke stated, "we expect revenue to be up 12% to 14%... Adjusted EBITDA margin... is expected to be up 500 to 550 basis points in the second quarter of 2026 compared to the first quarter of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q1 2026 were $409.1 million, up approximately 2.3% year-over-year. The Hardware Solutions segment delivered $189.1 million in net sales, up 2.4%, but with adjusted EBITDA down to $4.5 million due to $3 million in Monterrey-related costs. Extruded Solutions revenue was $139.8 million, essentially flat, with adjusted EBITDA at $20.9 million. Custom Solutions sales grew 4.8% to $89.1 million, with adjusted EBITDA declining to $4.6 million.

  • Cash used by operating activities was $20.2 million; free cash flow was negative $31.5 million. Liquidity stood at $331.6 million, and the leverage ratio was 2.8x net debt to last 12 months adjusted EBITDA. Management expects "our leverage ratio to increase slightly in Q2 but we also believe we will exit 2026 with a net leverage ratio closer to 2.0x."

Q&A

  • Kevin Gainey, Thompson, Davis & Company: Asked about margin improvement in Extruded Solutions. Scott Zuehlke explained, "the Extruded Solutions segment... have historically our most profitable products" and noted the segment’s operational model relies on fewer, larger plants driving margin.

  • Gainey further explored Custom Solutions’ revenue growth. George Wilson highlighted success in cabinet components, stating, "we've been able to secure some new market share as people have in-sourced product from overseas... our team has done a very good job of... providing a wide array of products just in time as they need it."

  • Gainey inquired about industry sentiment at the builder show. Wilson responded with, "there's guarded optimism... everyone believes in the long-term view and the optimism that exists in the housing market... the indicators are there that housing is in demand."

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company: Questioned margin expansion cadence. Zuehlke indicated, "the main driver for the second half '26 versus the second half '25 is... the Monterrey [plant] stable, we should not see that impact in the second half of this year."

  • Romero also asked about cash conversion cycle improvements and debt strategy. Zuehlke stated, "you'll see from us really over the next probably two to three years is a significant improvement in getting that cash conversion cycle for the legacy time of business down."

  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group: Asked about spacer growth drivers. Wilson said, "the driver in the growth of all spacer markets... is definitely being driven by the demand... on the performance -- the thermal performance of Windows" and expects it to remain a growth driver.

  • Ramsey asked about bundling and segment strategy. Wilson explained, "it's really hard to go to your customers and try to offer advantages of bundling when you have a product line that was not performing... We're at that point... there will be a time and a place in the near future."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was cautiously optimistic but probed for details on margin recovery, cash flow, and segment-specific growth, especially regarding Monterrey’s resolution and new initiatives.

  • Management maintained a measured and constructive tone, expressing confidence in operational stabilization and future growth, yet frequently referenced macroeconomic uncertainty and a "guarded optimism."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s sentiment shifted from caution and remediation to a more stable outlook, while analysts maintained a similar level of cautious inquiry.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance for 2026 is consistent with the prior quarter, reiterating expectations of flat overall performance with a stronger second half. The outlook language remains cautious but is now supported by the stabilization of the Monterrey plant.

  • Strategic focus shifted from addressing operational disruptions to executing growth initiatives and pursuing operational efficiencies. The previous quarter focused more on remediation and managing through headwinds, while this quarter emphasizes market development and new product launches.

  • Key metrics, such as Q1 sales and EBITDA, reflected seasonal and macroeconomic headwinds as anticipated, while Custom Solutions showed relative strength through market share gains.

  • Management’s tone has become more constructive as operational issues have been resolved, and analysts continued to focus on operational improvement, margin expansion, and cash generative capacity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, low consumer confidence, and geopolitical tensions are highlighted as persistent risks.

  • Management cited rising energy prices as a new headwind but noted that inflation and labor costs have started to moderate.

  • The company is closely monitoring developments in the Middle East for possible impacts on demand, raw material pricing, and shipping rates for international hardware business.

  • Seasonality and cash flow timing, particularly related to legacy Tyman business, are acknowledged as ongoing challenges.

Final Takeaway

Management at Quanex Building Products emphasized operational stabilization, particularly in the Hardware Solutions segment, and maintained guidance for fiscal 2026 with a focus on margin expansion, debt reduction, and targeted growth initiatives. Despite ongoing macroeconomic headwinds and a cautious consumer environment, the company is optimistic about demand improvements as confidence returns and is prioritizing new market opportunities, product innovation, and operational excellence to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NewLake Capital signals disciplined strategy and 85% payout ratio amid federal cannabis reform momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gordon DuGan, Chairman, stated that NewLake Capital delivered a strong performance in 2025, generating $51 million in revenue, $44 million in AFFO, and returning $1.72 per share in dividends from $2.09 per share of AFFO. He highlighted the company's ongoing focus on disciplined risk management, re-tenanting, and sourcing quality opportunities. DuGan commented, "Our measured pace of origination reflects intentional discipline as we navigate the current environment and position the company for future growth once reforms materialize." DuGan noted President Trump's executive order to accelerate rescheduling cannabis to Schedule III as a significant policy development, but emphasized the necessity of DOJ follow-through.

  • Anthony Coniglio, President and CEO, reported that fourth quarter results were in line with expectations, with AFFO at $0.51 per share and an 85% AFFO payout ratio. Coniglio stated, "Our full year results exceeded those of 2024, which is especially notable in a market where competitors reported year-over-year declines in both revenue and AFFO." He shared that the company closed two transactions with Cresco Labs and partnered with Curaleaf and C3 to optimize property performance and reduce risk. Coniglio also described the successful transition of a San Diego dispensary to Wellgreens, resulting in a 5-year lease extension and a reduction in exposure to the cannabis operator from 9% to 8% of annualized base rent.

  • Lisa Meyer, CFO, stated, "For the full year of 2025, our portfolio generated total revenue of $51.1 million, representing a modest 1.9% increase from $50.1 million for the full year of 2024." Meyer attributed revenue growth to acquisitions, improvement allowances, and rent escalators, partially offset by vacancies. She highlighted a strong balance sheet with $433 million in gross real estate assets, $7.6 million in debt, and $106.3 million in available liquidity.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a cautious stance, indicating no major changes to underwriting or capital allocation until federal reforms are finalized. Coniglio noted, "We're not taking these reforms for granted nor are we adjusting underwriting or capital allocation based on anticipated policy outcomes." He also said that the ban on hemp-derived THC, set for full implementation on November 12, 2026, could help stabilize pricing and support operator revenue growth in the second half of 2026 and into 2027.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q4 2025 was $12.3 million, reflecting a year-over-year decrease driven by vacancies. Net income attributable to common stock for the quarter was $6 million or $0.29 per share. AFFO for the fourth quarter was $10.6 million or $0.51 per share, representing a 3% decline from the same period in 2024. The company declared a Q4 dividend of $0.43 per share, paid in January 2026, with an AFFO payout ratio of 85%. As of year-end, leverage was 1.6% debt to total gross assets, with no maturities until May 2027.

Q&A

  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates: Asked about the impact of the Ayr security deposit applied in Q4 and modeling the first quarter impact. Lisa Meyer responded, "The $408,000 represents a little over 1.5 months of rent... No, it would just be the $408,000. We already have the property carrying costs on balance sheet... those will just continue to roll forward."

  • Zuanic inquired about the pace of re-tenanting vacant properties and whether management shared the positive sentiment seen elsewhere. Anthony Coniglio explained, "We're very cautious about this industry... while we have seen a modest pickup in interest in the vacant properties, we're just going to continue to be very cautious." DuGan added, "It's tough getting a lease across the line on any of these. So... we're very appropriately cautious about announcing anything ahead of getting something done."

  • Zuanic asked about the operational status of properties leased to Cannabist and Acreage. Coniglio replied, "From what we know all those properties are operational... all of our tenants are in compliance with their leases. Nobody is in a default position when we sit here today."

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital: Asked if the lease terms for the San Diego asset re-tenanted to Wellgreens matched previous terms. Coniglio clarified, "It's not a 5-year lease. It's a 5-year lease extension... Rent was not adjusted."

  • Kucera asked about strategic expansion outside cannabis. Coniglio said, "We continue to evaluate all opportunities to deliver growth for shareholders... when we think there's an opportunity for good risk/reward, we'll present it to our investment committee and ultimately, the Board for approval." DuGan noted, "The positive momentum from the regulatory standpoint has probably raised the bars in a subtle way for doing something outside of it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on risk exposures, tenant status, and re-tenanting progress, with a generally neutral but cautious tone. Their questions sought clarity on modeling, market sentiment, and operational visibility.

  • Management maintained a disciplined and slightly cautious tone throughout, emphasizing risk management and readiness for reforms. Coniglio and DuGan both highlighted discipline and caution, stating, "We're very appropriately cautious," and, "we're not taking these reforms for granted."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts remain cautious, but the tone this quarter reflected modest optimism due to policy momentum and improved operator interest.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management language shifted from emphasizing portfolio stability and risk mitigation in Q3 to highlighting policy developments and proactive tenant transitions in Q4. The Q4 call included more detailed discussion of legislative changes and their potential impact.

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on tenant risks, portfolio vacancies, and the outlook for federal reform. This quarter saw more focus on operational transitions and potential effects of upcoming regulatory changes.

  • Key financial metrics showed modest year-over-year growth in revenue and AFFO, but Q4 results reflected a small decline due to property vacancies.

  • Management’s confidence level is consistent, with discipline remaining central despite policy progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing industry headwinds, including capital scarcity, operator execution, and lack of federal reform. Coniglio said, "the broader cannabis landscape remains challenging without federal reform, and we continue to proactively manage risk."

  • The company continues to face vacancies and is cautious about re-tenanting timelines. Management is monitoring tenants like Cannabist, which is in forbearance with creditors, but currently all tenants are in compliance.

  • There is no regulatory investigation affecting NewLake, and management emphasized transparency in communications.

Final Takeaway

NewLake Capital Partners enters 2026 with a strong balance sheet and low leverage, maintaining a disciplined approach to risk and capital allocation amid a challenging cannabis industry backdrop. Management remains cautious but sees potential upside from pending federal reforms and the ban on hemp-derived THC, while reiterating that underwriting standards will not change until policy changes are finalized. Portfolio performance was steady, supported by proactive tenant management and a continued focus on protecting shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Frontera Energy says Parex Resources offers superior proposal for Colombia assets
data.description:
Frontera Energy (FECCF) said late Thursday that its board has determined a binding ​offer received from Parex Resources (PARXF) to acquire its Colombian upstream ‌operations is a superior proposal to another bid previously received from GeoPark (GPRK).

GeoPark (GPRK) announced an agreement in January to acquire all of Frontera's (FECCF) oil and gas ​exploration and production assets in Colombia for $375M, but Parex's (PARXF) offer ​would involve the same assets as GeoPark in a $500M ⁠cash deal, including the assumption of ​debt and a $25M contingent payment.

GeoPark (GPRK), which would be paid a $25M break fee from Frontera (FECCF) if the previous deal falls through, has been advised of the superior offer determination and has until March 12 to amend the terms of its agreement ​if it chooses, the company said.

Frontera (FECCF) is one of the largest private ​producers in Colombia, with a portfolio of 17 exploration ​and production ⁠blocks; the company reported annual production of 38,934 boe/day at the ⁠end ​of last year's Q3.

data.title: U.S. stocks' decline persists as nonfarm payrolls contract, oil prices accelerate
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages continued to decline on Friday after nonfarm payrolls unexpectedly contracted in February, and oil prices continue to rise.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.1%.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls dropped by 92,000 in February, vs. the 60,000 consensus—a sharp reversal from the 126,000 jobs added in January, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

“January bounced on the stronger side, February bounced on the weaker side. This is a shift towards labor market worries but only a very small shift. Mostly it is what a world with usual volatility around low supply growth will look like. Fed should still wait and see,” said Jason Furman, economist and former U.S. chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers.

In addition, U.S. retail sales declined by 0.2% MoM to $733.5B in January, vs. the 0.4% fall expected, the U.S. Census Bureau reported.

Oil prices (CL1:COM) climbed to $90/bbl on Friday as investors weighed escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East following military confrontations between the U.S. and Iran. Brent futures (CO1:COM) rose to $92.

Moreover, U.S. President Donald Trump said the U.S. should play a role in selecting Iran’s next leader after the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in recent U.S.-Israeli strikes. He also said there will be no deal to end the U.S.-Israel war against Iran without an "unconditional surrender."

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dropped 3 basis points to 4.11%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) lost 6 basis points to 3.53%.

data.title: Three small-cap energy stocks flash 'A' profitability amid $90 Oil surge
data.description:
The energy sector drew intense investor focus this week as crude oil prices vaulted above 90 per barrel for the first time since October 2023, propelled by U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran that rattled global supply chains.

The conflict has severely disrupted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a key chokepoint that handles roughly 20% of the world’s daily oil consumption, and renewed attacks on tankers and infrastructure have amplified fears of prolonged supply shortages and higher fuel costs worldwide.

Against this tense backdrop, a closer look at the small-cap corner of the energy market shows that only three names currently earn a top-tier A profitability grade: KNOT Offshore Partners LP (KNOP), Deep Yellow Limited (DYLLF), and FLEX LNG (FLNG).

In this framework, a profitability grade of A signals exceptional financial performance, with a company consistently generating superior margins, returns on capital, and earnings quality versus industry peers. Firms in this top cohort typically combine strong cost discipline, high asset utilization, and resilient cash flows that can better withstand commodity price swings and geopolitical shocks. 

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Gold Reserve, other miners eye return to Venezuela after receiving U.S. OK to negotiate
data.description:
Gold Reserve (GDRZF) said Thursday the U.S. Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control issued a 30-day license allowing the company and other miners to negotiate with Venezuela's government for a potential return to the country.

U.S. Secretary of the Interior Burgum arrived in Venezuela on Wednesday with representatives of more than two dozen mining and minerals companies, including Peabody Energy (BTU), Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and Coinbase (COIN), according to The Wall Street Journal.

"Venezuela is a rich, rich country filled with both oil and gas resources but also rich in critical minerals," Burgum said after his visit, which he said could represent billions in investment and thousands of high-paying jobs for the country. "The opportunities now are greater than ever."

Gold Reserve (GDRZF) hopes to resume mining in Venezuela after the government seized control of its Brisas gold mine in 2009.
